## Phase 2: Execution

| Impact Variable (IV) Name | Assessment State | Main Content |
|--------------------------|------------------|--------------|
| China Economic and Diplomatic Influence in the Gulf (CEDIG) | High | <P1>China's economic and diplomatic influence in the Gulf is projected to reach a High state by the resolution date, as Beijing establishes significant economic footholds and diplomatic channels that create strong resistance to an exclusive US-centric alignment. Recent evidence shows bilateral trade between China and Saudi Arabia exceeded $107 billion in 2024, with investments rising 30% to $8.2 billion [1]. Furthermore, strategic complementarity is deepening through massive infrastructure projects like the China-Saudi Industrial Park and Kuwait's Mubarak Al-Kabeer Port [3].</P1><P2>The High assessment is more appropriate than Medium because China's outreach is no longer just moderate hedging; it involves structural integration in green energy and technology, with projected investments in Gulf renewables reaching $50 billion by 2030 [2]. This level of influence constitutes a substantial barrier to a US-centric pivot, as Gulf states increasingly view China as an enduring strategic partner despite regional conflicts [34].</P2> |
| European Defense Autonomy and NATO Relevance (EDANR) | Medium | <P1>European Defense Autonomy and NATO Relevance is projected to be in a Medium state, where Europe achieves credible defense planning and moderate growth, thereby reducing the opportunity cost of a US reallocation toward the Gulf. European NATO defense spending is surging toward a projected €800 billion by 2030 under a 3.5% GDP benchmark [6]. Initiatives like the European Sky Shield demonstrate a move toward enhancing strategic autonomy in air and missile defense [4].</P1><P2>A Medium state is justified because while Europe is making modest improvements in self-reliance, it remains heavily dependent on the NATO framework for core security guarantees [5]. The trajectory suggests that while Europe will not achieve full strategic independence, its increased readiness and spending will eventually allow the US to shift resources without leaving a total security vacuum in the European theater [49].</P2> |
| Global Oil Market Dynamics and Fiscal Revenue (GOMDFR) | High | <P1>Global Oil Market Dynamics and Fiscal Revenue are projected to reach a High state, as elevated energy prices generate substantial windfall revenues that boost the capacity for robust foreign investment. While some forecasts suggest potential surpluses in 2026 [8], the ongoing regional instability and threats to the Strait of Hormuz have caused significant price surges [9]. A closure of the Strait could leave a 7 million bpd gap, creating a severe oil shock that would drastically increase revenues for producers with bypass capacity [10].</P1><P2>The High assessment is appropriate because the structural volatility in the region provides a high probability of sustained price premiums. This fiscal abundance is a necessary engine for the integration of US and Gulf economies, as it provides the capital required for the massive strategic asset acquisitions envisioned in the pivot [44].</P2> |
| Gulf Sovereign Wealth Fund Investment Capacity (GSWFIC) | Extreme | <P1>Gulf Sovereign Wealth Fund Investment Capacity is projected to reach an Extreme state, characterized by unprecedented capital availability and a strategic directive to anchor in the US system. Recent announcements indicate a massive scale of intent, with Saudi Arabia and the US discussing investment plans nearing $1 trillion [39]. Furthermore, the UAE has committed to a $1.4 trillion investment framework over ten years, specifically targeting US assets [40].</P1><P2>The Extreme state is justified as Gulf SWFs have become the "default buyers" of Western AI infrastructure, with collective commitments to US technology reaching approximately $2.5 trillion [41][42]. This level of liquid reserves and strategic mandate ensures the maximum capacity to meet and exceed the $500 billion commitment threshold required for the strategic pivot [15].</P2> |
| Israeli Concessions on Palestinian Statehood (ICPS) | Negligible | <P1>Israeli Concessions on Palestinian Statehood are projected to remain Negligible, acting as a total political barrier to the regional normalization required for the pivot. Current trends show a rejection of Palestinian independence post-October 7, with international observers warning that Israeli actions in the West Bank constitute de facto annexation [16][18]. There is little evidence of substantive policy shifts toward sovereignty transfers [17].</P1><P2>The Negligible assessment is appropriate because conditions do not even meet the Low threshold of symbolic gestures. The current Israeli political environment and security doctrine prioritize colonial control over concessions, effectively stalling the normalization process that is a prerequisite for the formal US-Gulf strategic realignment [27].</P2> |
| Iran Nuclear Ambitions and Regional Behavior (INARB) | High | <P1>Iran's Nuclear Ambitions and Regional Behavior are projected to be in a High state, creating an urgent necessity for a formal containment framework. Reports indicate Iran possesses over 400kg of highly enriched uranium, sufficient for multiple nuclear devices if the doctrine shifts [20][22]. The loss of IAEA access following strikes on enrichment sites has further heightened proliferation fears [19][21].</P1><P2>The High state is justified because Iran's technical advancements and aggressive regional operations necessitate a formal security response from the US and its Gulf partners [44]. This behavior acts as the primary catalyst for the "grand bargain" and the formalization of a security architecture targeting Iran containment [47].</P2> |
| Iran Proxy Activity and Maritime Security (IPAMS) | Extreme | <P1>Iran Proxy Activity and Maritime Security are projected to reach an Extreme state, where systematic maritime aggression necessitates a maximal containment architecture. Recent escalations include coordinated cluster bomb strikes by Hezbollah and renewed Houthi threats to Red Sea shipping [24][26]. The near-total closure of the Strait of Hormuz during recent conflicts demonstrates the extreme crisis levels proxy activity can reach [39].</P1><P2>The Extreme assessment is appropriate because the frequency and disruption of proxy attacks on shipping and infrastructure have generated sustained pressure for a formal US-led security framework [46]. This level of threat structurally negates the possibility of a benign regional posture and forces a permanent reallocation of US assets to the Gulf [44].</P2> |
| Saudi Arabia Accession to Abraham Accords (SAAAA) | Low | <P1>Saudi Arabia's Accession to the Abraham Accords is projected to be in a Low state, with only tentative diplomatic signaling occurring through exploratory channels. While a "grand bargain" is discussed at the executive level, public opposition in Saudi Arabia remains near 99% following regional conflicts [28]. Normalization is increasingly viewed as "slipping away" due to the lack of progress on Palestinian statehood [27].</P1><P2>A Low state is more accurate than Negligible because the relationship persists as a covert partnership, but it falls far short of the Medium state's active negotiation process [29]. The unmet conditions regarding Israeli concessions and the high domestic political risk for the Saudi leadership suggest that formal treaty accession remains a distant prospect [30].</P2> |
| Saudi Arabia Domestic Political Stability (SADPS) | High | <P1>Saudi Arabia's Domestic Political Stability is projected to remain High, enabling the state to confidently execute transformative diplomatic initiatives. Progress reports on Vision 2030 show significant milestones in labor reforms and women's empowerment, which are being highlighted internationally [31][32]. The regime maintains strong governance and tracks progress through sophisticated priority scorecards [33].</P1><P2>The High assessment is justified because the institutional stability provided by the current leadership allows for the pursuit of long-term strategic goals despite regional shocks [43]. While risks exist from top-down reforms, the unassailable nature of the current governance structure provides the capacity to sustain a full strategic pivot [9.1].</P2> |
| Saudi Iran Detente Stability (SIDS) | Low | <P1>The Saudi-Iran Detente Stability is projected to be Low, as the detente remains highly fragile with frequent setbacks. While Saudi diplomacy has favored coexistence to thwart wider war, the underlying rivalry remains unresolved [35]. China's role as a broker provides some endurance, but the relationship is characterized by managed tensions rather than rapprochement [34].</P1><P2>The Low state is appropriate because the detente has not evolved into a robust or institutionalized framework. The ongoing proxy activities and nuclear advancements by Iran sustain a moderate but inconsistent need for US-led security guarantees, preventing the detente from negating the premise of a US-Gulf alliance [44].</P2> |
| US Congressional Ratification of Security Treaties (UCRST) | Medium | <P1>US Congressional Ratification of Security Treaties is projected to be in a Medium state, where bipartisan support is emerging for limited security cooperation. The complex political environment following regional conflicts is "testing U.S. strategy," but there is a clear recognition of the strategic necessity of Gulf alliances [44]. Ratification of a formal framework is feasible but remains potentially fragile due to domestic polarization [38].</P1><P2>A Medium state is justified because while there is no overwhelming bipartisan commitment for automatic ratification, the Executive Branch's push for a "grand bargain" and major non-NATO ally status for Saudi Arabia provides a plausible path forward [39]. This state reflects a transition from skepticism toward a more structured legislative consensus [49].</P2> |
| US Domestic Political Environment (UDPE) | Low | <P1>The US Domestic Political Environment is projected to be in a Low state, with significant domestic friction and partisan volatility creating a weak environment for long-term commitments. Recent political developments show intense polarization, with Democrats invoking the 25th Amendment to curb executive actions while Republicans remain divided [36][37]. Public frustration with both major parties is high, as evidenced by polling on "double-haters" [38].</P1><P2>The Low assessment is appropriate because the extreme polarization and isolationist sentiment in some quarters create a hostile environment for new foreign entanglements [48]. This volatility makes the mandate for a Gulf-oriented strategy inconsistent, potentially blocking the institutionalization of the pivot [49].</P2> |
| US Executive Branch Strategic Prioritization (UEBSP) | Extreme | <P1>US Executive Branch Strategic Prioritization is projected to reach an Extreme state, with a comprehensive, institutionalized policy making the Gulf the primary theater of US strategic focus. The 2025 National Security Strategy explicitly prioritizes targeted partnerships and burden-sharing, moving away from traditional Eurocentric focuses [48][49]. The designation of Saudi Arabia as a major non-NATO ally is a definitive signal of this doctrinal shift [39].</P1><P2>The Extreme state is justified by the institutionalization of commercial diplomacy and the explicit prioritization of Gulf alliances over European commitments in the latest strategic documents [50]. This drives a permanent reallocation of assets and signals a sustained shift in national security doctrine that transcends individual administrations [49].</P2> |
| US Regulatory Environment for Foreign Investment (UREFI) | High | <P1>The US Regulatory Environment for Foreign Investment is projected to reach a High state, as streamlined approval processes facilitate the rapid absorption of Gulf capital. The 2025 National Security Strategy emphasizes deregulation and tax cuts to make the US the premier destination for business and investment [49]. This policy shift is specifically designed to attract massive influxes of sovereign capital [48].</P1><P2>The High assessment is appropriate because the current trajectory points toward a standardized and predictable framework that removes previous barriers to Gulf-owned strategic infrastructure [49]. This environment is essential for accommodating the trillion-dollar investment frameworks currently being negotiated with Gulf partners [40].</P2> |
| US Technology Transfer and AI Export Policy (UTTAEP) | High | <P1>US Technology Transfer and AI Export Policy is projected to reach a High state, with proactive policies facilitating significant AI and semiconductor integration with Gulf states. The US has committed to offering technology sharing to partners who align on export controls, positioning itself as a primary provider for the Gulf's AI ambitions [49]. This is evidenced by the massive $2.5 trillion in tech-focused commitments from Gulf funds [41].</P1><P2>The High state is justified because the US is using technology transfer as a tool for "geopolitical protection," ensuring that Gulf states remain anchored in the US technical ecosystem [41]. This liberalized policy environment is a key pillar of the broader strategic realignment, enabling deep technical alignment in critical sectors [15].</P2> |
